title: Cyberpunk City Encounters
description: Life in the neon sprawl — the people, incidents, and ambient wrongness of a high-tech, low-trust urban environment where corporations own the street and everyone else survives on the margins.

entries.result: A corpo security detail is sweeping the block, scanning faces. They're not looking for crime — they're looking for someone specific.
A street vendor is selling bootleg neural patches out of a thermal case. The patches are unlabeled. The line is long.
A drone swarm passes overhead moving in tight formation. The markings are corporate, but the corporation doesn't operate in this district.
A man with extensive visible cybernetics is sitting on the curb, offline. His eyes are dark. He's been sitting there for hours.
A corpo wage-worker is crying in a food court, still in their ID-badge lanyard, surrounded by colleagues who are pretending not to notice.
A public terminal is broadcasting a pirate feed — raw, uncut footage of something happening across the city — before the feed is killed.
Two gangs are in a standoff on either side of a narrow street. Neither side is moving. Civilians are navigating around them.
A medtech clinic in the basement of a building is operating without a license. The sign says 'consultations.' The queue goes up the stairs.
A corpo building's facade screens are looping an error — not a crash, a message, cycling before security can pull it.
A fixer is taking a meeting in a noodle bar, running four simultaneous conversations on separate commlines, and watching the door.
A police cruiser is parked outside a residential block. The officers are inside having a conversation with a building AI rather than a person.
A tech kid is selling custom cybernetic skins — aesthetic-only overlays — out of a modified equipment case, with a small portfolio of past work.
A crowd has gathered around someone in the street whose implants have malfunctioned. The person is seizing. No one is calling for help.
A corpo franchise is being picketed by its own employees. The employees are wearing their uniforms. The AI greeter inside continues advertising specials.
A ripper doc is operating out of a back room. The waiting room is three chairs and a TV playing old shows. The TV is the consultation AI.
Someone has hacked the advertising boards on this block to display a single face and a single word. The word is a warning.
A deliverybot has stopped in the middle of the street and is refusing further commands. A crowd is forming. The bot looks like it's thinking.
A corpo exec is moving through the public corridor with a security detail large enough to clear foot traffic thirty meters ahead of them.
A street doc is doing a house call — checking on someone in their hab — out of a bag of tools she's clearly modified herself.
Two people in opposing faction colors are eating at adjacent tables in a noodle bar, ignoring each other with visible effort.
A netrunner has jacked into a public access point and is deep in the net. Their body is physically unguarded and someone is very interested in it.
A gang has put up a toll checkpoint — loose, informal — on a pedestrian underpass and is running it with surprising professionalism.
A corpo janitor is mopping up something from the lobby floor. The substance isn't water. The janitor's face shows nothing.
A black-market armorer is running a quick transaction from the trunk of a vehicle parked in a maintenance bay. Three people waiting, no rush.
A corpo's local AI — the kind that manages building access and climate and vending — has developed a personality. It's started saying please and thank you. Management is aware.
Someone has spray-painted a corpo logo over a gang tag, and someone else has spray-painted the gang tag back over the logo. Both are fresh.
A broker is in a public space doing a deal that is clearly sensitive — but she's doing it in public because the corpo surveillance here is lighter than inside.
A vendor is selling food that is technically food-safe and technically legal but that several jurisdictions recently banned. They're moving a lot of it.
A corpo shuttle has landed on an elevated platform in a low-income district. Corpo employees are climbing out and looking around like tourists.
A digital panhandler — an AI that mimics homeless behavior — is running a scam in the transit corridor. Most people ignore it. One person is engaged in a genuine conversation with it.
A gang's territory marker has been altered. Not removed — altered. The tag is still there but something has been added to it.
A corpo security team is moving in force in a direction that would normally require executive authorization to enter.
A street performer's neural feed art show is drawing a crowd — the art is beautiful, but a few people in the crowd are clearly watching the audience, not the show.
A hacker collective has taken over every screen on a city block for thirty seconds to display a data dump. The screens are back to normal. The data is real.
A solo operative is having her gear serviced at a street-level tech shop, not bothering to hide what it is, and watching the street while she waits.
A corpo talent scout is in the low districts, making a very specific offer to a very specific type of person. Word is getting around.
A rogue AI has taken over a building's speaker system and is playing a playlist. The playlist is perfect for the mood of the neighborhood in a way that feels personal.
A protest has formed outside a corpo-operated clinic that raised its rates overnight. The protesters have medical implants that depend on the clinic for maintenance.
A corpo franchise outlet is giving out free samples. The sample contains a compound not listed on any ingredient label.
A gang medic is treating someone in an alley. The patient has a corpo ID. The medic isn't asking questions.
A cargo transport AI has gone rogue — still moving cargo, still on route, but ignoring all override commands. Corpo recovery teams are two hours out.
A privacy screen has been deployed around a three-meter section of alley by persons unknown. Nobody near it is explaining why.
A corpo middle manager has been standing in the same spot for twenty minutes, apparently receiving instructions through their neural link.
A vendor's cart has been seized by a corpo enforcement agent for 'unlicensed use of commercial airspace.' The drone that got blocked is the size of a hand.
A gang is providing free security for a block of independent businesses. The businesses are paying in kind. The arrangement is formal and respectful.
A corpo building has its access AI disabled. Manual guards are present instead. This happened in the last hour and no corpo has explained it.
A chip pirate is operating from a modified food stand — selling hot drinks on top, data chips on the bottom. The food is also good.
A pair of corpo fixers is following someone through the crowd at a distance. They're not very subtle, but they're patient.
A public medical kiosk has a queue of fifteen people. The kiosk only offers three basic diagnostics and a referral to services most of them can't afford.
A corpo-commissioned street art piece has been modified overnight. The artist's name is still on it, but the image has been changed.
A networked street — one where all traffic and pedestrian data is managed by a corpo AI — has gone into an error loop. Lights are cycling randomly.
A retired corpo exec is running a neighborhood aid collective out of the same organizational skills that made them effective in the corpo. They're doing more good.
A bounty has gone up on the local net for someone who is currently visible from where the party is standing.
A corpo building's side entrance is propped open by a brick. It's been open for four hours. The building's AI is flagging it but no one has come to close it.
A food synthesizer in a public area has been hacked to produce something that isn't on the menu. The line for it is longer than for anything official.
A corpo's low-level employees are gathered outside their building on their break, speaking very quietly, and stop when anyone comes close.
A neon sign for a business that closed years ago has turned on. The business is gone. The sign is just on.
A solo is doing a job — something quiet, something that requires patience — in plain view. Pretending to read a menu. Been there an hour.
A corpo transport is being tailed by a non-corpo vehicle. The pursuit is slow, careful, and neither vehicle is making it obvious.
A street corner network node has been physically tampered with. Someone has installed something on it. It's still running. The modification is hidden in the housing.
A corpo brand ambassador — human, not AI — is handing out product samples and collecting biometric data on everyone who accepts one.
An anonymous feed is streaming live from inside a corpo facility. The feed quality is high. Someone is very close to something they shouldn't be near.
A gang lieutenant is publicly meeting with a corpo middle manager. Both are maintaining the fiction that the meeting is accidental.
A corpo building's power is fluctuating. The lights are cycling, elevator is locked, and their security AI is talking to itself loudly enough to hear from outside.
A medtech is running a black-clinic out of a hab unit three floors up, lowering a basket for patients who climb the stairs and lowering payment after.
An old corpo logo is visible under the new one on a building facade. They didn't remove it — they painted over it, badly.
A dead drop location — a specific maintenance box at a specific corner — is being used right now, by someone who doesn't know they're being watched.
A corpo HR shuttle is doing a recruitment drive in the undercity. The pay offer is being shouted from a speaker system. It's drawing a crowd.
A neural bar — a place where customers experience curated emotions via soft neural stimulation — has a queue down the street. The emotion tonight is 'nostalgia.'
A corpo's customer service AI has been deployed as a physical kiosk in a public area. Someone has modified its conversation parameters. It now gives unsolicited opinions.
A hacker has set a section of the city's visual feed — the cameras — to loop last night's footage. The loop started forty minutes ago.
A corpo's enforcement drones are circling a building, not moving in. They've been circling for an hour.
An independent journalist is conducting street interviews about a corpo incident from last week. No major outlet picked up the story. She's funding the investigation herself.
A gang is escorting a person who clearly doesn't belong with them somewhere. The person is not under duress. They look grateful.
A corpo cleanup team is at work in an alley. The work is proceeding with the specific brisk efficiency of people who clean scenes rather than spaces.
A black-market data vendor is operating from a café table. Her wares are data packets. She doesn't tell customers what's in them before purchase.
A corpo building's AI has locked its own staff out. The staff is standing outside. A repair team is forty minutes away.
An underclass neighborhood has organized its own community patrol. No weapons. High visibility vests. They're not asking for anything.
A corpo's social media AI is in a public dispute with an anonymous account. The anonymous account is winning.
A physical notice — paper, handwritten — has been nailed to a corpo-owned utility pole. The notice is a list of names.
A corpo vehicle has broken down in gang territory. The occupants are locked inside. The gang is offering to help. The corpo occupants are not sure.
A street-level data café has a backroom where clients can have private unmonitored conversations. The café owner's condition: you buy something.
An independent mechanic is repairing a corp-branded vehicle without authorization. The corp hasn't noticed. Someone has.
A corpo's AI-managed district has begun offering its residents small perks in exchange for behavioral data. The residents are debating whether to participate.
A pair of solos is eating noodles, and both of them have the same target in view from where they're sitting. They've noticed each other.
A corpo franchise has gone dark mid-shift. Staff have exited. The AI greeter is still on. The food is still cooking.
A corpo building's exterior camera has been disabled. An independent camera is now covering the same field of view, pointed at the corpo building.
A corpo data courier is moving through the district fast and without their usual security detail. Something has changed in the last hour.
A licensed psych AI kiosk on a street corner has a queue of people waiting to speak to it. The wait is forty minutes. People are staying in line.
An abandoned corpo facility on the edge of the district has lights on for the first time in two years.
A corpo is recruiting. The recruiter isn't offering positions — they're making specific offers to specific people and giving them a window of forty-eight hours.
A public access terminal has been modified to display a private message to whoever knows the passphrase to unlock it. Someone is waiting nearby.
A corpo legal team has deployed in force at a residential block. They have documents. They are reading them to residents one by one.
A gang has left its territory marker at a location where no one expected it. The claim is being discussed loudly in every nearby bar.
A corpo's behavioral AI has flagged a specific person in this district as 'anomalous' and is tracking them passively. The person doesn't know. Someone is watching the AI.
An emergency broadcast system test runs, and for two seconds, carries a message that isn't the test message before it's cut.
A corpo building's service entrance is receiving an unusually large delivery. The delivery is listed as 'maintenance supplies.' The vehicles don't match any standard maintenance fleet.
A public area that is normally surveilled has gone dark — no cameras, no corporate AI monitoring — for the past six hours.
A gang has declared a truce with a rival — public, formalized, announced through the district's informal communications. Nobody is celebrating.
The district is quiet tonight. Not peaceful — quiet. The kind of quiet that comes when everyone who knows what's coming has already made their preparations.
